Definition: A logically isolated virtual network in AWS where you launch resources — you define IP address ranges (CIDR blocks), subnets, route tables, internet gateways, and security controls for complete network isolation.

A method for allocating IP addresses and routing — expressed as an IP address followed by a slash and prefix length (e.g., [IP_ADDRESS]), determining the size of the network and number of available host addresses.
The IP address range assigned to a VPC — primary CIDR can be /16 to /28, and up to 5 secondary CIDRs can be added. CIDR blocks cannot overlap with peered VPCs or connected on-premises networks.

Subdivisions of a VPC's CIDR block mapped to a single Availability Zone — public subnets have routes to an Internet Gateway, while private subnets route through NAT Gateways or have no internet route.
An isolated data center (or group of data centers) within an AWS Region — subnets are AZ-specific, and deploying across multiple AZs provides high availability and fault tolerance.
A set of rules (routes) that determine where network traffic is directed — each subnet is associated with one route table, and routes specify destination CIDR blocks and target gateways/endpoints.
A horizontally scaled, redundant VPC component that enables communication between instances in a VPC and the internet — attached to a VPC and referenced in route tables for public subnet routing.

A managed, highly available network address translation service that enables instances in private subnets to initiate outbound internet connections while preventing unsolicited inbound connections from the internet.

An EC2 instance configured to perform NAT for private subnet instances — a legacy approach replaced by NAT Gateway, but still relevant for scenarios requiring custom routing, port forwarding, or bastion host functionality.
A static, public IPv4 address that you can allocate and associate with instances or NAT Gateways — it persists until released and can be remapped between resources for failover scenarios.

A virtual network card attached to an instance in a VPC — each ENI has a primary private IP, optional secondary IPs, an Elastic IP, security groups, a MAC address, and a source/destination check flag.

VPCs support dual-stack (IPv4 + IPv6) networking — IPv6 CIDR blocks are /56 for VPCs and /64 for subnets, all IPv6 addresses are globally unique and public, and Egress-Only Internet Gateways provide outbound-only IPv6 internet access.
A VPC component that allows outbound IPv6 traffic from instances to the internet while preventing inbound IPv6 connections — the IPv6 equivalent of a NAT Gateway for IPv4.

A networking connection between two VPCs that enables routing traffic between them using private IP addresses — non-transitive (A↔B and B↔C does not mean A↔C), cannot have overlapping CIDRs, and works cross-account and cross-region.

A regional network hub that connects VPCs, VPN connections, and Direct Connect gateways through a central point — supporting transitive routing, route tables, multicast, and inter-region peering for simplified network architecture.

A routing table associated with Transit Gateway attachments that controls traffic flow between connected networks — supports route propagation from VPCs and VPNs, and static routes for custom routing policies.

Inter-region connectivity between two Transit Gateways using AWS's private global network — enabling transitive routing across regions without VPN or Direct Connect, with static routes configured between peered TGWs.

A technology that provides private connectivity between VPCs, AWS services, and on-premises networks without exposing traffic to the public internet — traffic flows through ENIs using interface VPC endpoints.

A VPC component that provides private access to S3 and DynamoDB through route table entries — traffic stays on the AWS network, no internet gateway or NAT required, and endpoint policies control access.

An ENI-based endpoint powered by AWS PrivateLink that provides private connectivity to AWS services, SaaS applications, and your own services — uses private DNS and security groups for access control.

The ability to expose your own application (behind a Network Load Balancer) as a PrivateLink service that other AWS accounts can connect to via interface endpoints — enabling private, secure service sharing without VPC peering.

Design patterns for organizing workloads across multiple VPCs — including shared services VPC, inspection VPC, egress VPC, and workload VPCs connected via Transit Gateway for centralized networking and security.

Using AWS Resource Access Manager to share subnets across accounts within an AWS Organization — enabling multiple accounts to launch resources into shared VPC subnets while maintaining separate account-level security boundaries.

A dedicated private network connection from your on-premises data center to AWS — providing consistent network performance, reduced bandwidth costs, and private connectivity that bypasses the public internet.

A globally available resource that enables you to connect your Direct Connect connection to VPCs in any AWS Region (except China) — supporting multiple VPC associations and Transit Gateway associations.
A logical interface on a Direct Connect connection — Public VIF accesses AWS public services, Private VIF accesses a single VPC, and Transit VIF accesses VPCs through a Transit Gateway.
A virtual interface that provides access to all AWS public services (S3, DynamoDB, EC2 public endpoints) over a Direct Connect connection using public IP addresses and BGP public ASN.
A virtual interface that provides private connectivity to a single VPC (via Virtual Private Gateway) or multiple VPCs (via Direct Connect Gateway) using private IP addresses and BGP private ASN.
A virtual interface that connects a Direct Connect connection to a Transit Gateway via a Direct Connect Gateway — enabling access to thousands of VPCs across multiple regions through a single VIF.
A logical interface that bundles multiple Direct Connect connections (same bandwidth, same location) into a single managed connection using LACP — providing increased bandwidth and link redundancy.

Dedicated connections (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps) provide a physical port at a Direct Connect location. Hosted connections (50 Mbps to 10 Gbps) are provisioned by AWS Direct Connect Partners and share partner infrastructure.

AWS recommends four resiliency tiers: Maximum (separate connections at separate locations), High (two connections at two locations), Development (single connection with VPN backup), and Critical (dual connections at dual locations with diverse providers).
Combining Direct Connect with an IPsec VPN tunnel over the same connection to achieve both private dedicated bandwidth and encryption in transit — the VPN runs over the Public VIF to encrypt traffic.

IEEE 802.1AE MAC Security providing Layer 2 encryption on dedicated Direct Connect connections (10 Gbps and 100 Gbps) — encrypting data at the Ethernet frame level between your router and the AWS Direct Connect endpoint.

An encrypted IPsec tunnel between your on-premises network and AWS VPC over the public internet — supporting two tunnels per connection for redundancy, BGP or static routing, and up to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel.

The AWS-side VPN endpoint attached to a single VPC — terminates Site-to-Site VPN connections and Direct Connect Private VIFs, supports route propagation to VPC route tables, and enables path selection with BGP.

The on-premises side of a Site-to-Site VPN connection — represents your physical or software VPN device with its public IP address and BGP ASN, configured to establish IPsec tunnels with the Virtual Private Gateway.
A hub-and-spoke VPN architecture where multiple customer gateways connect to a single Virtual Private Gateway — enabling encrypted communication between remote branch offices through the AWS VPN hub.

A VPN connection that uses AWS Global Accelerator to route traffic from the customer gateway to the nearest AWS edge location — reducing latency and jitter by leveraging the AWS global network instead of the public internet.

A managed client-based VPN service that enables secure remote access to AWS resources and on-premises networks — supporting OpenVPN-based clients, Active Directory/SAML authentication, and split-tunnel or full-tunnel routing.

The routing protocol used by Direct Connect and Site-to-Site VPN to dynamically exchange routing information between your on-premises network and AWS — supporting AS path prepending, MED, and local preference for path selection.
A BGP technique to influence inbound traffic routing by artificially lengthening the AS path on less-preferred routes — making the path appear longer so BGP prefers the shorter alternative path.
Tags attached to BGP route advertisements that control route propagation scope — AWS uses communities to control Direct Connect route advertisement scope (local region, continent, or global).
The routing rule where the most specific (longest prefix) route in a route table wins — a /32 route takes priority over a /24, which takes priority over a /16, regardless of other route attributes.

A feature that captures IP traffic metadata (source, destination, ports, protocol, action, bytes) for network interfaces in a VPC — published to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Data Firehose for analysis and troubleshooting.

A VPC feature that copies actual network packet data (not just metadata) from ENIs to monitoring appliances — enabling deep packet inspection, content analysis, and threat detection for network forensics.

A central management console for your global network — providing topology visualization, route analysis, and monitoring for Transit Gateways, Site-to-Site VPNs, Direct Connect, and SD-WAN integrations.

A VPC feature that performs automated path analysis between two endpoints in your VPC — identifying configuration issues (security groups, NACLs, route tables) that block connectivity without sending actual traffic.

A feature that identifies unintended network access paths in your VPC — analyzing security groups, NACLs, route tables, and VPC peering to detect resources that are reachable from the internet or other untrusted networks.

CloudWatch provides network-specific metrics — VPN tunnel status (TunnelState), Direct Connect connection state (ConnectionState), NAT Gateway metrics (BytesOutToDestination, PacketsDropCount), and Transit Gateway metrics (BytesIn/BytesOut).
A query engine for analyzing VPC Flow Logs and other log data — enabling SQL-like queries to identify top talkers, rejected connections, traffic patterns, and anomalies across network flows.

A feature of Network Manager that provides a global view of your private network — monitoring Transit Gateway connections, VPN tunnels, and Direct Connect links with automated alerts for connectivity issues.
The DNS resolver for VPCs that handles DNS queries for resources within the VPC (private hosted zones) and forwards queries to public DNS — the foundation for hybrid DNS architectures.

Inbound endpoints allow on-premises DNS resolvers to forward queries to Route 53 Resolver for AWS-hosted domains. Outbound endpoints allow Route 53 Resolver to forward queries to on-premises DNS for corporate domains.

Conditional forwarding rules that specify which DNS queries are forwarded to on-premises DNS servers via outbound endpoints — matched by domain name (e.g., corp.example.com → on-premises DNS server IPs).

A managed service that filters and blocks DNS queries from VPCs to known malicious domains — using AWS-managed threat intelligence lists and custom domain lists to prevent DNS-based data exfiltration and C&C communication.

Automated checks that monitor endpoint health (HTTP/HTTPS/TCP) and trigger DNS failover — supporting calculated health checks (combine multiple checks), CloudWatch alarm-based checks, and latency-based routing decisions.

DNS routing strategies — Simple (single resource), Weighted (percentage-based distribution), Latency (lowest latency region), Failover (active-passive), Geolocation (user location), Geoproximity (resource proximity with bias), and Multivalue (multiple healthy IPs).
DNS zones that contain records for domains resolved only within associated VPCs — enabling custom DNS names for internal resources without exposing them to the public internet.

Configuration sets that control DNS servers, domain names, NTP servers, and NetBIOS settings for instances in a VPC — a VPC can only be associated with one DHCP options set at a time (default provides AmazonProvidedDNS).

A networking service that uses AWS edge locations and the AWS global network to route traffic to optimal endpoints — providing static anycast IP addresses, automatic failover, and reduced internet latency for global applications.

Global Accelerator optimizes TCP/UDP traffic at Layer 4 for non-HTTP use cases (gaming, IoT, VoIP) with static IPs. CloudFront is a CDN that caches HTTP/HTTPS content at edge locations for web content delivery.

Stateful virtual firewalls at the instance/ENI level — rules allow traffic only (no deny rules), evaluate all rules before deciding, support IP addresses and security group references, and automatically allow return traffic.

Stateless firewalls at the subnet level — rules are evaluated in order (lowest number first), support both allow and deny rules, apply to all instances in the subnet, and require explicit rules for return traffic.

Security groups are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), instance-level, allow-only rules evaluated together. NACLs are stateless (return traffic needs explicit rules), subnet-level, allow/deny rules evaluated in order.

A managed stateful firewall service for VPCs that provides deep packet inspection, intrusion prevention (IPS), TLS inspection, and domain-based filtering — deployed in a dedicated firewall subnet with Gateway Load Balancer integration.

Collections of rules for Network Firewall — stateless rules (5-tuple matching: source/dest IP, source/dest port, protocol) and stateful rules (domain lists, Suricata-compatible IPS signatures, standard rule groups).

A Layer 7 firewall that protects web applications from common exploits — attached to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, or AppSync, with managed rules for OWASP Top 10, rate limiting, IP reputation, and bot control.

Free DDoS protection automatically included for all AWS resources — protects against common Layer 3/4 attacks (SYN floods, UDP reflection, DNS amplification) with always-on detection and inline mitigation.

Paid DDoS protection providing enhanced detection, 24/7 DDoS Response Team (DRT) access, cost protection during attacks, advanced metrics, and protection for CloudFront, Route 53, ALB, NLB, EIP, and Global Accelerator.

A centralized security management service that applies WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, Network Firewall policies, security groups, and Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rules across all accounts in an AWS Organization.

A Layer 3 load balancer that distributes traffic to virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS, deep packet inspection) — using GENEVE encapsulation to transparently insert appliances into the traffic path without changing source/destination IPs.

A VPC endpoint that serves as the entry/exit point for traffic routed to a Gateway Load Balancer — placed in route tables to redirect traffic through inspection appliances in a centralized security VPC.

The process of decrypting encrypted HTTPS traffic at a load balancer or proxy — ALB terminates TLS and can re-encrypt to targets, NLB supports TLS passthrough to instances, and CloudFront terminates TLS at edge locations.

A service that provisions, manages, and deploys public and private TLS certificates — free public certificates auto-renew and integrate with ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, and NLB for encrypted communications.

Named sets of CIDR blocks that can be referenced in security group rules, route tables, and Transit Gateway route tables — managed prefix lists simplify management of IP ranges (e.g., AWS service IP ranges, corporate CIDRs).
The layered approach to network security — combining security groups (instance), NACLs (subnet), Network Firewall (VPC), WAF (application), and Shield (DDoS) for defense-in-depth network protection.

A managed load balancing service that distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets — supporting Application Load Balancer (Layer 7), Network Load Balancer (Layer 4), and Gateway Load Balancer (Layer 3).

A Layer 7 load balancer that routes HTTP/HTTPS traffic based on content — supporting path-based routing, host-based routing, query string routing, HTTP header routing, weighted target groups, and WebSocket connections.

A Layer 4 load balancer that handles TCP, UDP, and TLS traffic with ultra-low latency — supporting static IP addresses per AZ, preserving source IP, millions of requests per second, and TLS passthrough.

ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) with content-based routing and WAF integration. NLB operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) with static IPs, ultra-low latency, and source IP preservation — choose based on protocol and routing needs.

A feature that distributes traffic evenly across all registered targets in all enabled AZs — enabled by default for ALB (free), optional for NLB (charges apply), ensuring even distribution regardless of AZ target count.

A feature that binds a user's session to a specific target — ALB supports application-based cookies (AWSALBAPP) and duration-based cookies (AWSALB), while NLB uses source IP affinity for TCP connections.

The time ELB waits for in-flight requests to complete before deregistering an unhealthy or deregistering target — default 300 seconds, configurable 0-3600 seconds, preventing abrupt connection termination during scaling events.

Logical groupings of targets (EC2 instances, IP addresses, Lambda functions, ALBs) that receive traffic from a load balancer — each target group has its own health check configuration and routing rules.

Periodic checks that determine target availability — configurable protocol, port, path, interval, threshold, and timeout. Unhealthy targets stop receiving traffic until they pass the healthy threshold count.

A global content delivery network (CDN) that caches content at 400+ edge locations worldwide — reducing latency by serving content from the nearest edge location, supporting static/dynamic content, WebSocket, and real-time streaming.

Sources of content for CloudFront distributions — S3 buckets (static content), ALB/NLB/EC2 (dynamic content), MediaStore/MediaPackage (video), and custom HTTP origins (any web server with a public DNS name).

A security feature that restricts S3 bucket access to only CloudFront — replacing the legacy Origin Access Identity (OAI), supporting SSE-KMS encryption, all S3 regions, and S3 bucket policies for fine-grained access control.

Rules that define how CloudFront handles requests for different URL patterns — specifying origin, TTL, cache policy, origin request policy, viewer protocol policy, and Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Functions associations.

A reusable configuration that controls what CloudFront includes in the cache key (headers, cookies, query strings) and TTL settings — separating cache key configuration from origin request forwarding for optimal cache hit ratios.

A CloudFront feature that runs Lambda functions at edge locations in response to CloudFront events (viewer request, origin request, origin response, viewer response) — enabling request/response manipulation, A/B testing, and authentication at the edge.

Lightweight JavaScript functions that run at CloudFront edge locations for simple request/response transformations — faster and cheaper than Lambda@Edge, ideal for header manipulation, URL rewrites, and cache key normalization.

Access control mechanisms for private CloudFront content — signed URLs restrict access to individual files with expiration, while signed cookies restrict access to multiple files (entire website sections) using a single cookie.

A feature that encrypts specific form fields at the edge using a public key — ensuring sensitive data (credit card numbers) remains encrypted through the entire application stack until decrypted by the authorized component with the private key.
